note: Although the second line, Ahi baba, baba, baba, baba, has the meaning indicated, it seems to function as a string of vocables that fix the audience's attention.

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2, of couplets composed as praise subject/ modifier of subject, formed in aab by contrast between couplets whose lines all contain both nouns and verbs (a's: 3-3.1, 4-4.1) and a single couplet whose lines contain, respectively all verbs and no verb (b: 5-5.1).

This ten-line string of images (lines 3-5.5) announce the principal themes of the ballad.The Spotted-one is a leopard and killing one in the kingdom of Bugabo brought iron weapons from Itahwa (Itawa), an early iron-working site in Kymutwara kingdom (Schmidt 1978, 280). The poet praises himself with the image, measuring the power of his rhetoric by the dangerousness of the leopard. That the leopard's death transcended boundaries foreshadows events in the ballad, in which the events span kingdoms and planes of reality.

The bickering of women that quickly overturned their reign seems to refer to a legend or proverb, but I have not found one. The quick back-and-forth of this kind of verbal interaction appears immediately. Without pausing for breath, Kajango answers his wife's complaint (line 11.1).

Three images for emotions associated with material goods: They sigh covetously: Nyabeteka mountain is so hard a climb that women must jettison goods they cannot carry up. Lacking space for guests combines satisfaction at the size of attendance with worry about not having enough food and drink . The coffee packet is becomes imbued with the presence of a lover.
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines composed as direct object + single verb phrase (a's: 5.3, 5.4) and a line composed as direct object + double verb phrase (b: 5.5).

caption: Banana-bast bundles: small triangular kashankala, large cylindrical olushankala.

note: compound stanza, 2 + 8 + 2, formed by contrast between lines composed as locutive + "my husband"/ verb phrase (a's: 9-9.1, 13-14) and intervening lines (b's: 10-11.3); the latter comprise two stanzas in aabb (10-10.3, 11-11.3); the first of these is formed by contrast between two lines composed as "You're worth less than" + noun + modifier (a's :10-10.1) and two lines that cohere by similarly inflected verbs in initial position; the first line (11) of the second such stanza (11-11.3) both finalizes the previous two lines (10.2, 10.3), inscribing them in a pattern of aab, and initiates the new stanzaic pattern of aabb formed by contrast between two lines that cohere by repetition of "they'd carried so much they were throwing things down" (a's: 11-11.1) and lines that cohere by enjambment (b's: 11.2, 11.3); note how Kajango's immediate appropriation of his wife's words (line 11) into the stanzaic pattern formed by his own utterance makes her less sure of herself: she pauses after "my husband" (13-14) as she did not do before (9-9.1).

The beer jug named Half-full is used to brew beer in small quantities for household consumption. Its name suggests its frequent use. The jug at least provides something useful -- the wife asserts -- unlike her husband Kajango.

Doves are a useful symbol for marriage because they often appear in pairs walking along on the ground. As Hayas can figuratively see them, the female goes ahead as if leaving for her parents' home after a marital dispute, while the male follows after her cooing apologies and excuses. The dove at least provides some satisfaction to its mate, Kajango's wife suggests.
Beer: Kajango's request is the first appearance of a thematic sequence that begins at home and extends into the world of spirits.
compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between two couplets whose lines cohere solely by enjambment (a's: 13.1-13.2, 14-14.1) and one couplet whose lines cohere by a number sequence.
The figure of a woman carrying her husband rather than her child on her back is sometimes used to describe strange places; see The Tree Mwata:107.
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines composed as preposition + placename (a's: 17, 17.1) and a line with an initial verb (b: 17.2).
The Christian name George (line 17) and the reference to ethnic East Indians (line 28) suggest that the landscape envisioned is a contemporary one.
stanza in abbba formed by contrast betweeen lines that begin with "He took" (a's: 18, 18.4) and lines that begin with (or contain) "he went" (18.1, 18.2, 18.3).
stanza in abba formed by contrast between lines with initial verbs (a's: 19, 21) and lines without initial verbs (b's: 19.1, 20: no verb and direct object initial, respectively).
stanza in aaaba formed by contrast between lines with initial verbs (a's: 22, 22.1, 22.2, 24) and a single line without an initial verb (b: 23).
Not a belt refers to the road travelled. It may look like a belt, stretching off into the distance, but it takes effort to travel it.
Pained-one and Arouser-of-desire address a female member of the audience.
stanza in abba formed by contrast between lines that begin with the verb, "he took" (a's: 32, 35) and lines that have no verb (b's: 33, 34).

compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 1, formed by contrast between couplets composed as direct object (place) + verb + placename/ "he went" + placename (a's: 35.1-35.2, 36-36.1) and a single line with an initial form of the verb "to be" (b: 37).
verse passage in aba formed by repetition of a prepositional phrase (a's: 37.1, 44) with intervening lines of vocables, praise, and temporal description (b's: 38-41, 42-42.4, 43; respectively)
Creeper vine is an image used here both for a meandering journey and for love, sensations of which sinuously cover the body, as a lutontozi  vine covers the ground.
see note for 5.3-5.5.

Three images for emotions associated with material goods: They sigh covetously: Nyabeteka mountain is so hard a climb that women must jettison goods they cannot carry up. Lacking space for guests combines satisfaction at the size of attendance with worry about not having enough food and drink . The coffee packet is becomes imbued with the presence of a lover.
Mubunda is a region in the southern part of  Ihangiro Kingdom.
stanza in abab formed by contrast between lines of quoted questions (a's: 44.2, 45.1) and lines of quoted answers (b's: 45, 45.2).
stanza in abab formed by contrast between lines of quoted questions (a's: 46, 48) and lines of quoted answers (b's: 47, 48.1)
Lugaba, "Provider," is a term of respect often used for a king or nobleman.

caption: Spear (eichumu, below) and Hafted knife (omwolo, above)

note: Lujoge is a recurring name that apparently refers to Kajango, but I do not know its meaning.

compound stanza in aba, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by two line pairs of sentences composed as noun (or pronoun) + verb phrase/ verb initial...(a's: 49-49.1, 51-51.1) contrasted with one line pair with two sentences per line (b's: 50-50.1).
Enshankala plantains are evidently not the best. They were prescribed as punishment in The Place You Come From (line 256)
This line is part of a set epithets that the poet uses elsewhere as well to embellish the reference to the evening hours (Kaiyula:78 and Kachwenyanja:19) but with modifications to suit the context.
compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between two couplets of adverbial phrases (a's: 52-52.1, 52.2-52.3) and a single couplet of verb initial independent phrases.

Blood brotherhood is a form of fictive kinship between men. It is ceremonially created when each partner makes a small incision near his navel, places a drop of his blood on one half of a coffee berry, and gives it to his partner to eat. It was especially useful in establishing relationships of trust among trading partners.
The sun rises from the waters of Lake Victoria to the east.
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines that are repetitions and transformations of each othe (a's: 62, 63) and a single line of quoted speech (b: 64).

The poet elaborates this moment of discovery with conjectures about the animals that might be present along with their respective praise epithets. The bard Abdallah Feza employs the same technique at a crucial moment in Kitekere (lines 219-241).

compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by couplets composed as negative verb + animal name [/+] praisename (a's:66-67, 68-68.1; the former completes the formula once in two lines, the latter twice) contrasted with a couplet with similar composition but positive verbs (b: 69-69.1).

compound stanza 3 + 3 + 1, formed in aab by contrast between units composed of vocables and couplets (a's: 70-71.1, 72-73), in which lines of couplets cohere internally by composition as verb + weapon + "and" + weapon and as " I let fly"... respectively, contrasted with a single line composed as a double verb formation (b: 73.1).

In traditional belief, termite mounds may envelop the spirits of deceased nobles. Here the mound saves the endangered hunter as a mother would comfort a child - by hoisting him on her back and covering him with a carrying cloth.
stanza in abab formed by contrast between lines composed as "I see..." + verb phrase (a's: 78, 79.1) and lines composed as "I go and I" + verb phrase (b's: 79, 80).
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines composed as verb initial + body part (a's: 80.1-81) and a verb initial line composed differently.
stanza in xaab formed by contrast between lines composed as "I killed" + praisename (a's: 82.1, 83) and one line comprised solely of praisenames (b: 83.1).
stanza in abab formed by contrast between lines composed as double verb formations that include "cut open" (a's: 87, 88) and lines that refer to an arrow inside the animal (b's: 87.1, 88.1)
This is one of three kings of Ihangiro who are named Nyarubamba. There is an epic ballad by that name.
Fire-forbidden refers to the leopard, which is said to be afraid of fire.
The images in these two lines refer to the chicken, to whom King Nyarubamba may be related via totemic association.
stanza in abac formed by contrast between lines that begin with a 1st person aside in the distant past tense (a's:91, 92) and others not so composed (a: 91.1, c:93).
stanza in aba formed by contrast between lines that contain "slept away" (a's: 93.1, 94.1) and a line marked by 0verb.

stanza in axaaab formed by contrast between lines that begin with a double word formation (a's: 95, 96, 96.1, 97) and a line that begins with a single verb with a similar inflection (b:97.1); an aside is interpolated as the second line (x: 95.1).

mirror stanzas (98-102.1, 102.2-103.4) of three couplets each; 98-99 - cohere by marked use of distant past tense (also used in the final line of the figure, 103.4); 100 is an interpolated aside; 100.1-102 - cohere semantically as sentences about laughter; 102.2-103 - cohere semantically as sentences about trading items; 103.1-103.2 cohere semantically as stimulus and response; final couplets in both stanzas (102-102.1, 103.3-103.4) are composed as adverbial past perfect verb + ..."leaving the hearth space" / modified double verb formation (verbs in different tenses).
You kill me - with laughter, that is. More sarcastic bickering. But from what she is about to say, the wife clearly does not understand the value of the hard-won trophies.
mirror stanzas (111-112.2, 112.3-113.1, 114-116) of four lines each, composed as [initial] verb + placename/ initial verb + placename/ Aside/ initial verb + place description
Buzinja, home of the Bazinja or Bazinza people, is the kingdom to the south of Hayaland. Mubunda, where Kajango spent the night before killing the rhinoceros is not far away from there.
stanza in abba formed by contrast between lines with 0verb (a's: 117, 118) and lines with an initial verb (b's: 17.1, 17.2)
stanza in abba formed by contrast between lines that contain the verb "drink" (a's: 123, 126) and those that do not (b's: 124, 125)
Manueli's bar is near the place of the bard's performance.
stanza in aaaabb formed by contrast between lines composed as 0verb [...] + placename (a's: 127, 128, 129.1, 129.2) and lines composed as initial verb + "(road)" (b's: 130, 131)
stanza in aabb formed by contrast between lines composed as placename/description + verb + placename (a's: 131, 132) with lines composed as initial verb + "road" + "of" + placname.
The image of the spotted-one, leopard, refers to and praises the poet.
stanza in aabb formed by contrast between lines composed as initial verb "arrive" + placename (a's:137, 137.2) and lines composed as initial verb "go" + placename (b's:132.1, 132.3).

stanza in abxab formed by contrast between lines composed as adverbial past perfect verb initial + placename/description (a's: 138, 139.2) and lines composed as narrative present initial verb + placename (b's: 139, 140).
The house columns have no storage at the top because they are so high.
Ankole is a region in western Uganda renown for its abundance of cattle.

compound stanza in aab, 2 + 3 + 2, formed by contrast between linegroups that combine two interlocutor's quoted utterances (a's: 148-148.1, 149-149.1) and a linegroup that comprises the words of a single speaker (b:151-151.1).
Stanza in aab, formed by contrast between lines whose main verbs are in the near past tense (152, 153,153 and a line whose main verb is in the distant past (b's: 155).
Bugengele and Lyamaholo are villages near the place of performance.
A mouth can show cheer in adversity by smiling.
Creeper vine is an image used here both for a meandering journey and for love, sensations of which sinuously cover the body, as a lutontozi  vine covers the ground.
Falls on its back, a leather kilt works is an image that I have tried to interpret but cannot.
Floating plants, bwonge, are images of travel, I believe.
The Great-calfed one here refers to the fisher god Mugasha.
This is part of Kajango's self-praise (ebyebugo). It proclaims his virtue of self-knowledge: The person who knows himself does not eat as a child, that is, does not eat indiscriminately.

three mirror stanzas (167-169.1, 170-170.3, 171-172.1) formed semantically by compositional units of wife's action (167-167.2, 170, 171), husband's response (168.1, 170.1, 171.1), and an articulated reason for his response (169, 170.2-.3, 172-172.1); first and second stanzas begin with "on that day"; second and third stanzas share reasons.
stanza in aabbbbbb formed by contrast between lines with an initial verb (a's:174, 175) and lines with 0verb composed of plant names (b's: 175.1- 176.1).

caption: A woman's private vessel, about 6" tall, which she receives at marriage and uses for pre-lovemaking ablutions. It is praised here as Off-they-racebecause it heats the needed water so quickly.

note: Bloodbrother is used figuratively here, as it is in other places, to connote intimacy and solidarity.

This is similar to Kajango's self-praise quoted on line 161.1: "He-who-knows-himself eats not the food of children. . . " The contrast is between beer, the drink of adults, and sweet mash, ripe-banana juice, the drink of children. It is said that drinking sweet mash can make a grown man impotent.
Recall it was the wife herself who offered sweet mash to Kajango (on line 167.2)

compound stanza in aba, 2 + 1 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets composed as ..."the folding"/..."falling together" (a's:186-186.1, 186.3-186.4) separated by a single line that includes "to fold" but inflected in the subjuntive (b: 186.2).

The folding and the draw-in-wrestling are metaphors for positions in lovemaking. In the folding, lovers embrace while facing each other in a seated position, their bodies folded at the waist; it is said to be the position of young lovers. According to Haya wrestling rules, a real draw between men grappling with each other occurs if they fall together on their sides, and their shoulders touch the ground at the same time. In the tie-in-wrestling position, lovers embrace face-to-face lying on their sides, with the woman's right thigh beneath the man's left; it is said to be the position favored by married couples.

compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by couplets that cohere by enjambment and begin with an adverbial past perfect (a's: 190.1-190.2, 191-192) contrasted with a couplet that coheres by parallelism and repeats "I fainted" in initial position in each line (b:193-193.1).
stanza in abb formed by contrast between lines with a similarly inflected verb in initial position (b's: 195-195.1) and a single line with different, and marked, tenses (a: 194).
stanza in aaaab formed by contrast between lines that begin with a locutive (a's: 196-199) and one line that does not (b: 200).

This episode is the generically marked Call and contains the major themes of the ballad. It is an apparently unthinkable scene, one in which a child curses its own mother. But a moment's reflection in Haya belief reveals cultural dissonances in the apparent horror that assure all is well, or at least, not as serious as it might seem. Calling on a spirit to injure someone, as the child is doing, is called oku-tega, and one usually does this in secret, at the spirit's shrine, not in the open fields before others. Moreover, the spirits the child primarily calls upon first, Nyakalembe is a beneficent fertility figure. One could never curse with her. But the most significant dissonance is that, according to traditional belief, one cannot curse (oku-tega) a woman. This is because the curse is an instrument of traditional property relations, a guarantor of contracts and a reprisal for theft. But women are not party to this system, because in it, they cannot own property. In another old ballad, Kabundu gulikiile "the pleasing shortness," a man tries to curse a woman for breaking a promise, but is told by one spirit after another that it is not possible. Such a curse, as soon emerges, is the issue in this ballad as well, namely, whether Kajango has been cursed and fallen into a life-threatening state. But Kajango does not "eat" indiscriminately like a child, nor has he "eaten" anything from another, that is, he has not incurred any debts that would make him vulnerable to a curse. And so one who is versed in these matters knows even at this point that, no matter how dire the circumstances appear, Kajango has been cursed by no one, not even his wife, and will recover.

As he does elsewhere, the bard Muzee elaborates a panorama of possessing spirits, describing how their names and epithets vary with the geography of Hayaland. His poetry envisions a systematic set of underlying identities between overtly different spiritual manifestations in the named kingdoms and locations.
stanza in abba formed by contrast between lines composed as placename + "they call her" + spirit name (a's: 201, 202.2) and lines composed as "they call her" spirit name (b's: 202, 202.1).

Epithets for spirits generally refer to aspects of their myths of origin, their characteristic effects on living humans, and/or their characteristic speech and behavior when they posses their spirit mediums. Many of the epithets are difficult for me to explicate with certainty.
stanza in abbbbba formed by contrast between lines composed as placename + "she is" + praisename (a's: 203, 205) and lines composed as praisenames alone (b's: 204-204.3).

stanza in ababxxxx formed by contrast between lines composed as place descriptor + "among" + people descriptor (a's: 206, 208) and lines composed as "call her" + praisename (207, 208.1); plus four praisenames (x's: 208.1-209.1).

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2, each couplet begining with a subjunctive verb, formed in aab by two couplets whose lines cohere semantically, being comprised of sentences associated by topic (a's: 210-210.1, 210.2-210.3- spiritual disease and social ostracism, respectively) contrasted with one couplet (b: 211-211.1) whose lines cohere by enjambment in the concatenation of praisenames.
stanza in abab formed by contrast between lines comprised of praise of Kajango (a's: 213, 215) and lines comprised of the child's address to his mother (b's: 214, 215.1).

Ruler . . . and Straightener refer to Kajango in this context. They usually are epithets that praise a king, who judicially, ritually, or militarily has the power and the responsibility to set things straight. Here the image valorizes a husband, father, and head of a household.
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines composed as body part + "brought forth" + effluent (a's: 216, 217) and a line composed as body part + "was cold" (b: 218).

compound stanza in aba, 2 + 1 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets (a's: 221-221.1, 221.3-221.4, which cohere internally by parallel composition as ...potable liquid...+ verb, and body part + verb phrase, respectively) and a single intervening line (b: 221.2).
The mother responds to the child's putative curse by saying politely -- if that is possible -- the equivalent of "drop dead."
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines of quoted speech introduced by a locutive (a's: 223-223.1) and a line of quoted speech not so introduced (b: 224).
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines composed as verb initial + "my fingers together" (a's: 225, 225.1) and a line composed as verb initial + verb (b: 225.2).
Snapping the fingers by pressing middle finger to thumb, leaving the index finger loose and flicking one's wrist is a gesture of dismay.
Once legendary kings, Bachwezi became a pantheon of spirits who possess ritual specialists and give them powers to treat health and social problems.
A woman walking with her hoe pointed to the sun signals her distraught state and appeals to that spirit for help.

compound stanza, 1 + 7 + 2 + 4 + 1, formed in axbba by single lines composed as [verb] + "hoe" + "mine" + verb + "toward the sun" (a's: 230, 234.1) contrasted with linegroups composed as "when I did" + verb phrase/ independant clause (b's: 231-232, 233-233.3; the two-line composition of the former is expanded to four lines in the latter); to the first line (a: 230) is appended a list of seven praisenames (x's: 230.1-230.7).
They are parted, that is, Kajango's soul (omwoyo) and his body have separated.

compound stanza 2 + 2 + 2, formed in aab by contrast between couplets composed as ["when"] "I touched" + "on" + body part/ verb initial independent clause (a's: 235-236, 237-237.1) and a couplet composed as vocable/ noun (body part) initial independent clause (b's: 238-238.1).

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 1, formed in aab by contrast between couplets (a's: 239-240, 240.1-240.2, which cohere internally as question /answer and as expressions of wife's caring, respectively) and a single line (b: 240.3) that indicates the wife's readiness.

Women customarily wail in grief to respond to and to announce the death of a loved one. The wife carefully does not do this lest Kajango's soul be startled and flee and become permanently severed from his body.
Women moving around in stiff fiber work skirts is an image of self-conscious caution: they must move carefully lest the stiff skirts tip up to reveal their nakedness.

compound stanza in aba, 2 + 2 + 1 formed by contrast between verb initial narrative lines with unmarked (near past) tenses (a's: 241-241.1, 243) and verb initial lines of asides in marked (present and distant past) tenses (b: 424-424.1).
Twigs are read by a diviner as embodiments of the various forces and agents that affect a particular household.

compound stanza in aba, 1 + 4 + 1, formed by contrast between single lines composed as verb phrase + "all (kinds of) twigs" (a's: 245, 250, reversed order in the latter) and four lines (b's: 246-249) that are structured in turn as aaab by contrast between lines with initial nouns (a's: 246-248) and a line with an initial verb (b: 249).
Bwogi when alive was the son and successor of King Kalemela of Kymutwara. He died and became a possessing spirit. There are many different versions of his characteristic praise.

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2, formed in abb by couplets that cohere by parallelism (b's: 254.1-254.2, 255-255.1) - composition as verb phrase + sun phase (reversed in second line) and as placename + "it is said" + adverb, respectively - contrasted with a couplet that coheres by enjambment of placename appositives (a: 253-254).

two three-line mirror stanzas formed by composition as "he killed" + masculine direct object (256, 257) and ..."they did not eat"...(256.1, 257.1), and by semantic equivalence of acts of mourning (256.2, 258.1).
Kimuli was the first born of King Kalemela. His murder by jealous cowives sent Kalemela into a rage.
Kasana's gait, pouring himself along is stereotyped movement of members of the ruling clans.

compound stanza, 1 + 2 + 2, formed in abb by couplets composed as place or person designation + relative inflection of verb "to know"/ "call her" + praisename (b's: 271.1, 271.3) contrasted with a single line composed differently.

compound stanza, 1 + 4 + 4, formed in abb by contrast between a single line (a: 274) and two quatrains, each of which is structured in turn as abba - the first by contrast between lines composed as placename + "is" + spirit name (a's: 274, 275.2) and lines composed as "they call her" + spirit name (b's: 275, 275.1); the second by contrast between lines composed as placename + "is" + spirit name (a's: 275.3, 276) and lines composed as spirit name alone (b's: 275.4, 275.5)

verse passage of four sections (253-260.2, 262-270.3, 271-272.1, 273-286), each composed of: visiting a particular spirit medium (253, 262, 271, 273), praising the spirit (254-258.1, 262.1-270.1, 271.1-271.4, 274-277.3), doing the water oracle (259, 270.2, 272, 278-279, and learning the result (260, 270.3, 272.1, 285); two passages are interpolated - taking back a consultation fee (260.1-260.2) and lamenting (280-284.1); two lines complete the passage as a whole (285.1-285.2).
A cattle path is larger and better traveled than a footpath and one is more likely to be overheard there.
Not being able to mourn publicly suggests that the deceased was a secret lover. While not the case, the wife is questioned about this several times.
stanza in aabb formed by contrast between lines that have "eat" as their main verb (a's: 293, 294) and lines that have "settle up" as their main verb (b's: 295, 295.1)

compound stanza, 1 + 2 + 2, formed in abb by contrast between a single line (a: 296) and couplets (b's: 296.1-297, 298-299) that cohere semantically as requests for explanation (296.1, 299) based on observations of evidence (297, 298).
The string tie of the woman's work skirt is cutting into her flesh; this and other signs indicate that she has gone for a long time without relaxing and tending to her body at home.

three lines composed as ...item contiguous to body + verb final; initial words on each line create alternate patterns of abb (contrasting an initial verb with its absence) aba (contrasting an initial prepositional phrase with its absence) and aab (contrasting a term of address with its absence).

stanza in axxabb formed by contrast between a couplet whose lines cohere as 2nd person subjunctive verb initial (a's: 306,308) and a couplet whose lines cohere as third person indicative verb initial (b's: 309-310); interpolated within the first couplet is a pair of repeated, thematic lines (x's: 307-307.1).

These are fragments of familiar songs sung at weddings. The first is about the importance of maintaining the health and cleanliness of one's body. The second praises the bride on her procession to her husband's house. Here they are sung by disembodied spirits.
Ta (Mr.) Dionies was the host of the event at which the poet sang.
stanza in abba formed by contrast between lines composed as noun + verb (a's: 330.1, 330.4) and lines composed as 2nd person verb + noun (b's: 330.2, 330.3).

compound stanza, 2 + 12 + 1 + 2, formed in abxa by contrast between two couplets that constitute question and answer (a's: 331-331.1 and 339-340) and six intervening couplets of praise that cohere internally (except for 336.4-336.5) by various parallelisms (b's: 332-337.1); a single vocable line is interpolated between praise and answer (x: 338).
Mother cow's call praises a woman who has borne a child. She behaves differently when she has become a mother, as cows do.
Love, which has cast the wife into her present situation, is praised in formulaic language
This is part of Kajango's self-praise (ebyebugo). It proclaims his virtue of self-knowledge: The person who knows himself does not eat as a child, that is, does not eat indiscriminately.
stanza in aabb formed by contrast between a couplet that coheres by enjambment of apposition (a's: 344-344.1) and a couplet that coheres by close parallelism (b's: 344.2-345).

stanza in aabb formed by contrast between a couplet that coheres by enjambment and repetition of "search for" (a's: 346-346.1) and a couplet that coheres by parallelism and repetition of "go" (b's: 346.2-347).
Wamara is the spirit-king of the Bachwezi pantheon. His style of movement (347.2-.3) shows his high social standing. Ndahula is his father.
Hare is the avenger because, as the hero of many animal fables, he usually repays the wrongs done against him.

compound stanza, 1 + 2 + 2, formed in aab by contrast between lines that repeat "you will meet in there" (a's: 352.4, 353-354; the latter coheres internally by composition as place description + "you will meet there" + animal name + praise) and a final couplet whose lines are closely parallel (b: 355-356).
stanza in abcb formed by contrast between lines composed as ..."they decant beer [in brewing,] the spirits" (b's: 356.2, 358) and lines composed differently (a, c: 356.1, 359).
Balanzi are a class of spirits, like Bachwezi, but they do not possess people nor are they sacrificed to. They can, however, harm people.

caption: Eijungilo, fermentation vessel, used in beer brewing
Young men add water to a fermentation vessel.

note: mirror stanzas (360-365.1, 366-370.2) each formed by couplets that cohere by parallelism (361-362, 363-364, and 366-367, 368-369), each completed by couplets that cohere by enjambment of apposition (365-365.1, 370-370.1), while the passage as a whole is begun and ended by single lines (360, 370.2).

caption: Here a young man steps into a vat of banana-sorghum beer mash
Not a chameleon but brewer Fransi Kato in 1970
The funnel here is made of a calabash
Expressing beer mash from banana-straw mixture with a woven wooden sieve
Preparing the mash mixture on the sieve
Here the medium for expressing the mash is straw.

note: Beer seats seem to be analogical extensions of the seats in a lake canoe to something in a beer canoe, or fermentation vat, but I cannot identify them.

caption: A young man uses a rope tied beside the vat to steady his balance.

note: stanza in abba: presence of "do not fear"
A woman would protect her modesty by folding her raffia skirt and sitting down on her haunches.

compound stanza in aba, 2 + 2 + 2, (lines 376-378 are interpolated and do not figure in the larger structure, although the alternation of vocables recapitulates it); first and last couplets cohere by marked repetition, incremental and reiterative; the middle coupled coheres semantically as named speech and quoted speech.
mirror stanzas that share a final line; the first is the elaborated one
Muziba used here suggests that the crow is the clan-brother totem of the Baziba clan, but I cannot confirm this. Muziba can also refer to any citizen of the northern kingdom of Kiziba.
names for the other world structured as aaab quoted speech/ described action
Escort . . . Return . . . Tell him . . . These quoted commands suggest, with great economy, the coercive practices of a multi-tiered state bureaucracy.
Shumaalamu is the greeting that a grown, married man is privileged to use.
names for the other world alternately structured as aabb and abba; Nimwo initial/ omwo initial, and quoted speech/ praise for Wamala, respectively
Without disease [or] consultation fees describe those that suffered a sudden death.
a verse passage, it seems, made of three quatrains (389-91, 392-392.3, 394-395.1) and concluded by a couplet of incremental repetition (396-397)
stanza in abba presence of distant past/ absence

quatrain in abab; composed semantically: in the second line a greater wonder emerges from that inherent in the first; the second couplet coheres syntactically by all verbs in 3rd pers present continuitive or relative inflection
six lines that cohere as as abcabc (or 2 + 1 lines whose constituent elements cohere as abcabc)
stanza in abba cohering as presence/ absence of I saw in distant past; in aaba as presence or absence of /bona/ see; as aaab syntactally as phrase structure a is b/ subject verb object
compound stanza 3 + 2 + 2 in aab, the first two line groups structured as emerging wonder as in 399-402 above, the final couplet a pair of sentences directly describing wondrous equivalences

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 1, in aab coherent as description/ dialogue; the couplets cohere in parallelism as verb in 1st pers near past + noun; and begin + to greet + as men/ stop + to greet + as woman
stanza in aba by parallelism: he swore + praisename/ he swore truly
threelines that cohere as abb by parallelism

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2 + 2, in abba; a's are composed as address + question + address and address + answer; each of the couplets designated as b coheres by lexical repetition, of ibwa and ita, respectively and by parallelism
stanza in aab by semantics acts of exchange, by syntax 2nd person subjunctive/ 1st person subjunctive
Father-creator is used here as an exclamation in a one-line aside.
stanza in aab adverbial phrase/ main verb + object
Coffee berries are often presented to guests in a small saucer-shaped basket.

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2 + 1, aaab; a's are couplets composed as common object + they [convey] to me/ bodypart + of + person(s); finalizing line is three verb; note within a's is a aab structure, the constituent parts of first line of couplet are reversed in the final one
Mortal hosts often provide guests a small napkin of papyrus strands to dust off their hands in preparation for eating coffee berries.

compound stanza sturctured as aba by turns of conversation: Wamala/ wife; finalized by repeated couplet;440.1-.4 two couplets structured as abab semantically statement of place/ present spiritual condition of wife; first two couplets cohere semantically
Wembaga kinehe is said to be a greeting in the language of the Sukuma people, who live to the south of Hayaland.
stanza in aab, finalized in a line that repeats the principal verb and contains the marker "this day"

compound stanza in aaaaab, composed of couplets that are three times out of five expanded to 3 or 4 lines; they cohere by parallelism: double verb + noun + noun (praisename)/ May your husband double verb (verb derived from noun in first line); the final couplet shares none of this structure but transforms the noun into verb figure by making the noun initial in the first line (as opposed to (near) final in the a's) and the verb derived from it final in the second line; couplets are expanded by adding an additional line type (line 445) an additional line of praise (452) or two additional lines of praise with parallel syntax (449-449.1)
Sneezing and farting are signs of life.
These lines are quoted from a women's wedding song.
quoted song in aab pattern
The host was a teetotaler, a religious man, and feared, I think, the negative effect of beer on the poet's art.

complex stanza in abac: a's are couplets formed by incremental repetition (470-471 and 471.3-472); b's are 2 similarly constructed but singular lines (471.1-471.2); c is related to one of the b's by repetition

stanza in abbac: a's are related by repetition; b's are the list of healing items embedded in a variety of syntactic structures; c is the repetition of a familiar line, and like the c in the complex stanza directly above
This seems to be a false start.
The identity of the first-person narrative voice has shifted to Kajango.
stanza in aab by syntax one/ two fully inflected verbs, by semantics, action and response
stanza in aab by lexicon
compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 1, couplets defined by syntax (493-93.1, second person), lexical repetition 94-94.1.
three couplets that each cohere by parallelism and repetition, repetition, and parallelism, respectively
compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 1, number sequence, noun and praisename, praisename alone, in a sense based on phrasing 2 2 1.

mirror stanzas with one line kicker; two couplets + two couplets + added line; initial couplet in each stanza composed of adverbial past Perfect in second Person + near past second person; second couplet coheres semantically; the first couplet of the second stanza incrementally repeats and transforms the second couplet of the first stanza
Laughter in the nose is a sardonic gesture.
White and Big-spot are names for cattle based on their markings.
